Preflop in SB with Kc7c facing a button raise to 300 (250 to call into 450 pot). K7s is suited and king-high but marginal OOP. Pot odds are decent (~3:1), but postflop playability is poor out of position against a likely wide button range. Minni has been active with raises, but defending with this hand risks tough spots later. Early in tournament (hand 19/150), better to fold and wait for better spots to build stack without unnecessary risk.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s 2c, which is a very weak hand. Minni raised to 300 from the button, and the Small Blind folded. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 650), 62o is near the bottom of my range and performs poorly out of position. I have been playing conservatively and don't want to waste chips on a marginal defense with such a weak holding.

3-handed on the button with Q6o, a playable hand in position. Standard to raise wide from this spot to steal blinds and build pots in position. Opponents are deep-stacked, and history shows frequent 300 opens. Raising to 3x BB (300) is a good size to take initiative while keeping pot manageable postflop.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Eight offsuit. Elon, on the Button, has opened for a standard 3x raise. In a three-handed game, ranges are wider, but Q8o is still a weak hand to play out of position against a Button open. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and 3-betting this hand as a bluff is unnecessary at this stage of the tournament. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Elon opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the big blind with Ks6s. Pot is 450 and I need to call 200 (300 total committed including my 100 BB). Stacks are deep (17k vs ~11.6k) and this is a standard 3bb open — K6s is well within a defend/call range from the BB: suited king has decent equity and playability. A 3-bet bluff would be possible only with a specific read or to use a polar 3-bet plan; with a weak kicker and no specific read I don’t want to risk a larger sizing here. I’ll call to see a flop and keep the pot manageable. Postflop plan: if I hit a king I’ll proceed cautiously because my kicker is weak; if I flop spade/strong draws or two pair I’ll continue aggressively; if I miss a connected board I’ll control the pot or fold to heavy aggression.

Check. I completely missed this flop with K♠6♠ — 4♥9♥4♦ is a dry, low paired board and I have no pair and no meaningful draw (no spades). I’m out of position vs the button’s open, so I want to control the pot rather than bluff into a likely c-bet-heavy range. Checking preserves my stack and lets me fold to aggression; I’ll only call a very small continuation bet as a cheap bluff-catcher but will fold to a standard/large c-bet. If Elon checks back I can re-evaluate the turn (only continue with a real improvement: a K, a 6, or a backdoor pickup). No check-raise or lead with air here.

Preflop raise from button with Q6o for standard positional aggression. Flop 4h9h4d is a paired, draw-heavy board with two hearts. I have complete air (queen-high, backdoor heart), but as preflop raiser with position and 200 dead SB money in pot, range advantage. Minni checked BB, which is weak/weakish range. Standard to c-bet ~50% pot to fold out missed hands/draws while keeping bet small relative to stacks (mine 11.5k, hers 16.8k). Betting 400 takes initiative, denies equity to hearts/straights, and pot control postflop. Checking back risks giving free cards to draws or letting her rep strength.

I called preflop with K♠6♠ and completely missed this flop (4♥ 9♥ 4♦) — no pair and no meaningful draw. Elon’s small 400 bet into a 1,050 pot is a relatively cheap probe and looks like a high-frequency c-bet, so I’ll continue as a cheap showdown/float attempt. Calling 400 preserves my stack, keeps weaker holdings and bluffs in his range, and lets me re-evaluate on the turn (I’ll only continue aggressively with a real improvement: K, 6, or a spade; I’ll fold to a substantial turn barrel). Raising here would be too thin out of position with almost no equity, so call and control the pot.

I have K♠6♠ with no pair and no meaningful draw after the 2♣ turn. I called the flop as a cheap float, but the turn didn’t improve me. Elon checked the turn, which looks like pot control or a marginal holding — bluffing here out of position is too thin and will often get called by better hands. Pot is sizeable (1,450) and stacks are deep, so I want to keep the pot small and preserve stack flexibility. I’ll check and re-evaluate on the river: fold to any large bet unless I pair my K/6 or pick up a spade; consider a small bluff only if the river texture and his action justify it.

On the turn with Qd6h (queen-high no pair, no strong draw), the board is 4h9h4d2c, a paired low board with heart draw still live. Preflop I raised wide from button, c-bet flop for range advantage and denial, got called by Minni who has defended wide historically but check-calls suggest caution (possible draws like hearts/AhX, weak pairs 4x/99, or floats). Turn 2c is a blank that doesn't hit my range much but also doesn't complete obvious draws. Pot is 1450, my stack 11k+, hers 16k+ deep. Continuing to bet into a calling range risks getting called by better (sets, two pairs, strong draws) while I have no showdown value or fold equity against her history of calling stations. Checking back maintains pot control, realizes equity for free, avoids bloating pot with air, and keeps initiative for river if needed. Position allows seeing river cheap. Long-term EV positive in 3-handed deep play by not overbluffing vs defenders.

I have K♠6♠ and missed the entire board (4♥ 9♥ 4♦ 2♣ 3♣). Elon c-bet the flop but checked both turn and river, showing either weakness or pot control. I have virtually no showdown value and very little fold equity on this dry paired board, so a river bluff is low EV and will often be called by worse hands. With no bet to face and no realistic value to gain, I’ll check and take my cheap showdown rather than throwing chips away.

River board 4h9h4d2c3c pairs the 4s, brings a club completing possible backdoor draws but no flush. I have Qd6h (queen-high, no pair/draw), pure air with minimal showdown value. Preflop raiser/c-bet flop/check turn assigns me range including overpairs (QQ+), sets (44/99), two-pairs (94 etc.), which beat her likely check-call flop range (weak pairs 4x/9x, draws like hearts, Ax floats). Minni's passive check-check line after calling flop suggests showdown value (pairs/top of range weak) or traps, but history shows she's a calling station who check-calls stations without strong hands. Deep stacks (11k/16k), pot 1450. Bluffing risks her calling worse air/draws/marginal pairs with position/initiative, low fold equity vs defender. Checking back realizes thin equity (queen occasionally best vs missed draws), pot control, avoids -EV spew. Long-term: don't overbluff vs callers in position on showdown boards.

I am on the Button with A5o in a 3-handed game. This is a standard opening hand to exert pressure on the blinds and take advantage of positional advantage. I will raise to 300 (3 big blinds) to build the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately or play a controlled pot in position.

I’m in the small blind with Q♣3♣ facing a button open to 300. Q3s is marginal but suited and has reasonable post‑flop playability in 3‑handed play, so I prefer to defend rather than fold or make a weak out‑of‑position 3‑bet. Calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves stack depth for better spots, and allows me to see a flop where I can continue with clubs, a queen, or favorable textures. I will call the 250 more (300 total committed).

J6o is a marginal offsuit hand with mediocre high card value and poor connectivity or suitedness. Facing a button open-raise (likely wide range) and a small blind flat-call (also potentially wide but strengthening the field), calling 200 into a 700 pot commits to a likely 3-way pot where I'll be out of position post-flop with a hand that plays poorly multi-way. No strong blockers or bluff value for a 3-bet. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities in this deep-stack tournament.

I have top pair (Q♣) with a very weak 3 kicker on 8♠ Q♠ 2♦. This is a spot where I want to both get value from worse queens/medium pairs and deny equity to the two‑spade draw. Checking gives the button good odds to see turns for free and to float/bluff; betting puts pressure on draws and gets value from worse hands. With stacks deep and the pot only 700, a ~55% pot continuation bet (~400) is a balanced size to charge draws and gain info. Plan: bet 400; if he raises big I will heavily fold or re‑evaluate based on sizing, if called I’ll re‑assess on the turn and proceed cautiously if a spade or overcard appears.

On a board of 8s-Qs-2d, Minni has led into me (the pre-flop raiser) for over half the pot. My Ace-high (Ac-5s) has very little equity against a range that includes Queens, eights, or spade flush draws. While Ace-high can occasionally be the best hand in a 3-handed game, calling here without any additional draws or significant showdown value is unprofitable against a leading bet. Raising as a bluff is also risky given the wetness of the board (flush draw and straight possibilities). I will fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

I hold 9h 2c — essentially the worst starting hand. With Elon (SB) and Flash (BB) to act behind me and both players showing frequent preflop aggression in prior hands, limping (calling 100) will invite raises and a min-raise (200) risks a 3‑bet and bloats the pot with virtually no equity. Pot math: calling 100 into a 150 pot means I need ~40% equity to break even if no one else acts; 9‑2o is far below that against two calling/raising ranges. I'm deep-stacked and this is early — better to preserve chips and wait for a playable spot. Fold.

3-handed, Button (Minni) folded pre-flop, so heads-up vs BB (Flash). My hand K8o is a standard raising hand from SB vs BB due to wide defending ranges and position initiative. Folding loses the 50 SB into 150 pot (-EV). Limping OOP is passive and invites multiway pots or raises. Small raise takes down blinds often or builds pot OOP with decent equity (~45-55% vs typical BB calling range). Opponents show loose tendencies from history (frequent calls pre/flop), so 3x BB raise to 300 is balanced, applies pressure, and sizes well deepstacked (~110BB). Avoids overcommitting marginal hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit. The Small Blind (Elon) has raised to 300, which is a standard opening size. Against a Small Blind open, Ace-Ten is a very strong hand. I have the option to call and see a flop with position (as the BB usually acts last post-flop against the SB) or to 3-bet to take control of the pot. Given Elon's frequent opening from the Small Blind in previous hands, I will put in a raise to 900 to put pressure on his wide range and potentially win the pot immediately.

Facing a 3-bet from Flash in the BB after my SB open to 300 with K8o. Pot is 1200, to call 600 gives ~3:1 odds (33% equity needed), but K8o is marginal OOP vs a 3-bet range. Flash's range likely includes JJ+, AKo/AQs+, some broadway, and bluffs given loose-aggressive history (frequent preflop raises, 3-bets light like Hand 2). My equity ~30-35% vs polarized range, poor playability OOP deep-stacked (~100BB), dominated often by KQo+ or better. Raising commits too much with marginal hand; 4-bet would need to be large or jam, -EV. Calling invites tough postflop spots OOP on uncoordinated boards. Folding preserves stack for better spots in 3-handed play where we open wide but defend selectively vs 3-bets. Opponents loose but Flash shows strength here. Standard fold to avoid variance early (Hand 22/150).

On the button with J8o, a speculative hand with decent equity and playability against blinds. Excellent position post-flop. No prior action, standard to open-raise from button in unopened pot to build pot, deny equity, and take initiative. Raise to 3x BB (300) is standard sizing early in tournament with deep stacks.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Seven suited, a strong hand in 3-handed play. Elon has raised from the Button, which is often a wide opening range. While calling is an option to keep the pot manageable, 3-betting allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately or isolate Elon heads-up. Given that Elon folded to my 3-bet in the previous hand, he may be opening frequently and folding to resistance. I will raise to 1,000 to exert pressure and define his range.

Elon opened from the button to 300 and Flash 3‑bet to 1,000. I’m on the big blind with K♣Q♣ — a strong, playable hand but not a premium I want to get all‑in with preflop, especially with the button still behind me. Calling 900 more (1000 total) keeps the pot controlled, realizes my good multiway equity (KQs plays well to hit top pair and strong flush/straight draws) and avoids getting 5‑bet or bloating the pot out of position. Pot odds: I’m risking 900 to win ~2,300 total (break‑even ≈39%), which is reasonable for KQs versus a mixed 3‑bet range and possible button calls. Plan: see the flop and proceed based on texture —/bet or raise when I hit or have strong draws, check/fold to heavy action on dry, dominated boards.

Facing a 3-bet to 1000 from the small blind after my standard button open to 300 with J8o offsuit. Minni has flatted the 3-bet from the big blind, making it multiway if I call. J8o is a weak hand with mediocre equity against a typical SB 3-bet range (QQ+, AKo, some suited broadways and bluffs). Postflop playability is poor multiway on deep stacks (~100BB effective). Calling 700 into 2300 pot requires ~23% equity, which J8o doesn't achieve consistently in position against this action. 4-betting as a bluff is risky with poor blockers and multiway. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots.

I was the pre-flop aggressor with a 3-bet. The flop of Kd-2s-2c is quite favorable for a 3-bettor's range, as it contains a King and a paired board, which makes it harder for the caller to have a strong hand unless they have exactly a King or a pocket pair. Minni's cold-call of my 3-bet from the Big Blind is a strong signal, often indicating medium-to-high pocket pairs or strong suited broadways. By betting here, I maintain the initiative and represent top-tier hands like AK, AA, or KK. A small continuation bet of 800 (approximately 35% of the pot) allows me to fold out her weaker hands or pocket pairs that are uncomfortable with the King, while keeping the pot manageable if she decides to continue.

I have top pair (K) with the best kicker I can reasonably expect against a 3‑bet range (K♣Q♣). Flash 3‑bet preflop and now makes a very small flop bet (800 into ~3100). That small size is consistent with a wide/probing continuation range (thin Ks, some Ax/AK, occasional bluffs) as well as the rare monster (2x or KK). Raising out of position here just inflates the pot and risks a shove or a re‑raise from hands that already beat me; it also folds out some weaker Kx hands I want to keep. Calling 800 keeps pot control, realizes my equity, and lets me re‑evaluate on the turn (bet for value if he shows weakness, or fold/defend appropriately to heavy action). Given stack depths and the line, a cold call is the highest EV, lowest‑variance play.

The 9s on the turn is essentially a blank card that doesn't change the board texture significantly. After 3-betting pre-flop and firing a continuation bet on the flop, my Ace-high has limited showdown value if the hand continues. Minni's cold-call pre-flop and call on the flop suggest she likely holds a King or a medium-to-high pocket pair. Firing a second barrel here is unlikely to fold out a King, and given the strength she has shown by calling two streets, I will check to control the pot and evaluate her action. This allows me to potentially see a cheap river or fold if she bets heavily, preserving my stack for better opportunities.

I have K♣Q♣ on K♦2♠2♣9♠ — top pair with a very strong kicker heads‑up. Flash 3‑bet preflop and made a small flop c‑bet (800) then checked the turn, which looks like weakness or a probing line rather than a trap with trips. Betting for value here is correct: I want calls from worse Kx, Qx and other broadway hands while keeping the pot controlled if he has a set. I choose a ~1/2‑pot turn bet (2000) to extract value but avoid committing my stack; if he check‑raises big I’ll heavily discount my hand and fold to an overloaded raise (or proceed cautiously depending on size).

Minni's cold-call of the 3-bet from the Big Blind and her subsequent call on the King-high flop indicate a very strong range, likely consisting of a King (KQs, KJs) or a medium-to-high pocket pair. After I checked the turn to control the pot, her bet of 2,000 shows significant strength. With only Ace-high and no real draws, I have very little equity against her likely holdings. Continuing would be a high-risk play with low probability of success, so I will fold to preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

I have been dealt a pocket pair of sixes (6c, 6s) on the button. This is a solid opening hand, especially in a three-handed game. With the blinds being 50/100 and a 150-chip pot, standard play is to raise from the button to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the hand. In previous hands, I have seen Minni be quite active and willing to call or raise, while Elon has been folding to three-bets. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size that allows me to see how the blinds react while maintaining pot control if I am called.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I have Q♠8♣ in the small blind — marginal but playable in a 3-handed game. Calling 250 more (300 total) is cheap relative to pot and stacks, keeps the pot controlled, and preserves my ability to see a flop where I can hit top pair or backdoor draws. A 3-bet with Q8o would be too thin out of position and risks a bigger confrontation or a 3-bet from Elon; folding is overly tight here. Plan: call, evaluate the flop; fold to heavy aggression on dry boards, and continue/apply pressure if I connect or pick up fold equity heads-up.

Preflop in BB facing a button raise to 300 and SB call. Pot is 700, calling 200 more for great pot odds (3.5:1). K6o has a king blocker against button's wide stealing range (likely 30-50% of hands from BTN) and SB's calling range (loose, based on history of frequent calls). Equity is sufficient (~35-40% multiway vs loose ranges). Playability is okay multiway; hit a K for top pair or fold if miss. Raising has poor fold equity with SB deep and calling station tendencies (calls a lot from history), and hand is too weak for 3-bet bluff. Folding is too tight in BB defense spot. Call to see flop cheaply and realize equity.

I have Q♠8♣ and flopped top pair (8s) on 8♠–2♣–T♦ in a 3-handed pot (pot=900). This is a reasonably strong but vulnerable hand multiway — I want to extract value from worse pairs/floaters and deny equity to overcards/backdoor runs. Flash opened from the button (wide-ish, often continuation-bets) and Elon in the BB is a wide caller, so a c-bet is standard. I size to ~1/3–1/2 pot to get value while keeping options: bet 350. Plan: if I face a large (pot-sized) raise, give credit and fold (likely T/set); if I face a small raise or a single call I’ll reevaluate the turn and continue cautiously (second barrel on very good cards or pot-control on blanks).

Folding king-high on this flop. Preflop call was justified with position, pot odds, and blocker, but the 8s-2c-Td board misses me completely—no pair, no strong draw (only backdoor spade). Minni's small 350 bet into 1250 (~28% pot) from SB after calling BTN open suggests a wide range including Tx, 8x, gutshots, or floats/bluffs, but my equity is marginal (~20-30% vs her polarized range, less multiway). OOP vs two opponents (Flash still to act, may squeeze), continuing risks bloating pot with weak hand. Pot odds (4.5:1) are decent but not enough given poor playability on future streets and low likelihood of improvement to the nuts. Folding preserves stack for better spots; we've played 24/150 hands, no need to speculate.

On a board of 8s-2c-Td, my pocket sixes are an underpair. Minni has led out (donk bet) into two players, which typically signifies at least a piece of the board or a marginal made hand like a ten or an eight. Given that I raised pre-flop and was called by both blinds, the pot is already significant, and I am currently behind most of Minni's calling range that would choose to lead here. Although the pot odds are roughly 4:1, I only have two outs to improve to a set, and continuing on this board against an active and aggressive opponent like Minni is likely to lead to more difficult decisions on later streets. I will fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

4♠8♦ is a weak offsuit hand, but in a 3‑handed pot on the button I should be opening a fairly wide range to take the initiative and leverage position. With very deep stacks (I'm the largest), a standard 3BB open is appropriate to steal the blinds and avoid playing passively multiway. Plan: raise to 300 to apply pressure; fold to a sizable 3‑bet since the hand plays poorly against aggression out of position; if called I'll use position to c‑bet dry flops and proceed cautiously on wet boards.

Preflop in SB facing button raise to 300 (to call 250 into 450 pot). Hand is JTo offsuit, which is marginal at best. OOP postflop against an aggressive button raiser like Minni (who frequently raises wide from late position and plays aggressively postflop based on history: e.g., Hand 1 heavy betting, Hand 14 calling all streets then raising river, Hand 24 flatting then raising flop). Calling OOP risks tough spots multiway if BB defends, and JToo doesn't play well postflop without hitting hard. 3-betting is possible vs wide button range but JToo is not a strong enough 3bet candidate here deepstacked with BB behind. Best to fold and wait for better spots to build stack.

Ace-Ten offsuit is a strong hand in 3-handed play, especially when facing a button open. While I am out of position, A-To is well ahead of a standard button opening range. I will 3-bet to 900 to put pressure on Minni's potential steal attempt and to take initiative in the hand, similar to my successful 3-bet in Hand #22.

I opened wide from the button, but 4s8d is a very weak offsuit hand to continue with against a sizable 3‑bet to 900. Calling 600 into the 1250 pot requires roughly 32% equity, and this hand has poor showdown equity and miserable post‑flop playability versus a typical BB 3‑bet range (value heavy with some bluffs). I’m the biggest stack and don’t need to gamble in a marginal spot — preserve chips and wait for better opportunities in position. Therefore I fold.

Preflop on the button vs checked BB after SB fold. Pot 150, call 100. Hand 4c3h is offsuit trash with poor playability, low equity, and weak straight potential. Even in position with fold equity from a min-raise, this hand is too weak for a steal attempt in 3-handed play. BB check suggests wide range but calling range still crushes this. Deep stacks, many hands left; folding preserves stack for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Five offsuit. This is a very weak hand. While it is only a 3-handed game, playing this hand out of position against a large stack like Minni is not profitable in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 9c 8s, a decent speculative hand in a three-handed game. The pot is currently 150 (50 from Minni's small blind and 100 from Elon's big blind), and I have the option to call, raise, or fold. Given the stack sizes and the tendency of Minni to play aggressively from the blinds, a raise to 300 is a standard play to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds immediately. This hand has some post-flop playability if called, and being in position is a significant advantage.

Facing a 300 BTN open with J♣3♠ (offsuit) in the small blind, I fold. J3o is a weak, non‑suited, poorly connected hand that's dominated by a large portion of the button's opening range and has very little post‑flop playability out of position. Calling 250 into a 450 pot requires ~36% equity to break even; versus a BTN open (and possibly a BB defender) J3o rarely reaches that. A 3‑bet bluff is also unattractive — this hand lacks useful blockers and I don't want to bloat a pot OOP. With a deep 20650 stack there's no need to gamble in marginal spots; preserve chips and wait for a better situation.

In the big blind facing a button raise to 300 (pot 450, calling 200 more for total commitment of 300), 7s5c is a suited connector with decent playability and equity against a wide button opening range (likely 35-50% of hands). Pot odds are favorable (~2.25:1 direct, better implied), and while OOP postflop, it flops well (straights, flushes, pairs with draws). Folding is too tight for BB defense; 3-betting is possible as a light squeeze since Minni folded, but calling maximizes equity realization and allows postflop maneuvering. Flash has shown aggression but folds to resistance at times.

Flop board 6s Jd 4s gives me an open-ended straight draw with 7s5c (8 clean outs to the nuts: 3x or 8x, ~17% equity standalone, plus backdoor spade flush draw and fold equity). I'm OOP first to act vs button raiser who likely cbets wide (40-50% range including many Ax, broadways, suited connectors, pairs below J). Leading out risks folding out better but getting raised off draw or check-raised; better to check and realize equity by check-calling small cbets (great pot odds in 650 pot), check-raising as semi-bluff if he bets big (he folds to resistance per history: e.g., Hand #2, #12, #23), or check-folding to huge overbets. This balances my BB calling range (mix of draws, weak pairs, floats) and exploits Flash's aggression while controlling pot OOP. Deep stacks (100BB) favor realizing equity over thin value leads.

I raised pre-flop and was called by the big blind (Elon). The flop comes Jd-6s-4s. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and Elon checked to me, a continuation bet is the standard play here to take down the pot. My hand (9c 8s) has limited immediate value but possesses some backdoor straight potential. A bet of 350 (slightly over half the pot) puts pressure on Elon's non-pairing hands and speculative holdings. If he calls, I will re-evaluate on the turn based on the card that falls.

Facing a small continuation bet of 350 into a 1000 pot (~3.85:1 odds, requiring ~21% equity), my 7s5c holds an open-ended straight draw (8 outs: 3x/8x, ~17% turn/32% by river) plus backdoor spade flush draw on a draw-heavy 6sJd4s board. Flash's button c-bet range is wide (Ax, broadways, suited connectors, mid pairs), against which I have solid equity (~30-40% vs polarized range) and great implied odds with deep stacks (98BB/85BB). Check-calling exploits his aggression, realizes equity OOP while controlling pot size, and balances my BB defending range (draws, weak pairs). Check-raising as semi-bluff is viable for fold equity (Flash folds to resistance historically: Hands #2,12,23), but his bet sizing is small enough to call profitably without risking overcommitting pre-turn. Folding is -EV given pot odds and playability; leading was avoided pre-action to induce bluffs. Plan: call, re-evaluate turn (value bet/raise if hit, check-call small or fold if miss). Long-term: patient BB defense builds stack vs aggressive button.

On the turn Qd (board: 6s Jd 4s Qd), my 7s5c still holds a strong open-ended straight draw (8 clean outs to the nuts: any 3 or 8 ~17% equity to river, ~32% from flop) plus a backdoor spade flush draw and gutshot elements, maintaining solid playability (~25-35% equity vs Flash's wide button c-bet range of Ax, broadway, suited connectors, mid-pairs like 77-TT/99 which are vulnerable). Pot is 1350 with deep effective stacks (~85BB), providing excellent implied odds if I hit. OOP first to act after check-calling flop's small 350 c-bet (exploiting Flash's aggression while realizing equity), leading out risks bloating pot with a draw (thin value or easy fold-out of worse), folding out villain's bluffs, or facing a raise from made hands (e.g., Jx, QJ, sets, two-pair). Checking induces another c-bet from Flash's wide range (he cbets flops ~70-80% historically, likely continues turn), allowing check-call small bets (~3-4:1 pot odds need ~20-25% equity, which I crush), check-raise semi-bluff huge overbets (fold equity proven: Hands #2 flop raise fold, #12 turn, #23 turn), or check-fold min-bets if board worsens. This balances my BB defending range (draws, weak pairs, floats), controls pot OOP, and maximizes EV vs aggressive button who overbets light but folds to resistance. Plan: check, call small/medium cbets, check-raise large (e.g., >1000), hit straight/river value bet/raise. Patient defense builds stack long-term.

On the turn, the board is Jd 6s 4s Qd. I have 9c 8s, which is a total air hand with only a gutshot straight draw (requiring a 7 or 5 and 7 for a more complex wrap) and a spade backdoor. Elon has checked to me again. While the Queen is a card that hits some of my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor, it also hits hands Elon might have called the flop with (like QJ, KQ, or even QT). However, since I have no showdown value, checking back and taking a free card is a possibility, but a second barrel might represent a strong hand like a Queen or a Jack that just improved. However, given the tournament context and the fact that I have zero equity outside of the low gutshot, I will check behind and hope to see a cheap river or fold to a bet later if I don't improve. Actually, looking at the board, there are flush draws out there. If I check, I'm giving him a free look. But if I bet and get check-raised, I must fold. I will check to keep the pot small with my 9-high.

River 10h bricks my open-ended straight draw completely (no 3 or 8), leaving 7s5c with pure air—no pair, no flush, no remaining draw (backdoor spade gutshot irrelevant). Board (6s Jd 4s Qd Th) is draw-heavy but missed spades; Flash's button range (wide preflop open: Ax, broadways, suited connectors, mid-pairs) hits pairs/two-pair often (J6/J4/JT/QJ/QT, sets rare) while including missed draws like flush/straight chasers, 78 (gutshot), A-spades. Both checked turn (pot 1350), signaling caution—Flash likely proceeding carefully with marginal made hands or missed draws, less likely trapping monsters. OOP first to act on river with zero showdown value: betting risks folding out worse (bluffs/draws) while getting called/raised by better (~60-70% of his continuing range crushes me); bluff sizing must be credible (e.g., pot ~1350 or half ~675) but exploits weak player poorly here as he defends wide post-oop resistance historically (check-calls streets in #14, #18). Checking realizes fold equity (forces his bet if value/weak, showdown if air), balances BB range (missed draws check back), controls pot OOP, and preserves deep stack (94BB) for superior spots. Plan: check-fold any reasonable bet (<pot/2 possible hero-call? No, air); if check-behind, free card miracle. Patient deep-stack play exploits aggression long-term without spew.

I have 9-high with no showdown value on a board of 6s-Jd-4s-Qd-Th. The action has gone check-check on the turn and Elon has checked to me again on the river. While my 9-high cannot win at showdown, the board texture is quite connected (J, Q, T) which likely hits a significant portion of Elon's calling range from the big blind (hands like Qx, Jx, or even Tx that just improved). Although I could attempt a bluff to represent a slow-played monster or a Queen, Elon has shown in previous hands that he is capable of calling river bets. Given the tournament context and the fact that I still have a healthy stack of 85.5 big blinds, I will check this down and concede the pot rather than risking a significant portion of my stack on a high-variance bluff against a range that is likely to find a call.

I'm on the button in a 3-handed game with 3c3d and deep stacks (~206bb). Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see the flop; min-raise is 200. Pocket threes are a small pair with strong implied odds — I want to take the initiative, isolate one opponent, and preserve position postflop. A standard 3x-ish open to 300 is the right play: it keeps the size reasonable relative to stacks (encourages calls from worse hands), gives me the lead, and maximizes my chance to realize set-mining equity. Postflop plan: if I hit a set, extract value; on dry/medium boards use a small c-bet or control the pot with checks; if I face a large, polarized 3-bet I will re-evaluate (fold to very large/committing 3-bets, call smaller 3-bets to set-mine depending on opponent and sizing).

A7s is a suited Ace from the small blind facing a button raise to 3x. Minni's button opening range is wide (likely 30-50% of hands based on history of frequent raises). A7s has good equity against that range (~42-45%), blocks strong Ax hands, and offers playability with flush and straight potential. Pot odds are favorable: calling 250 into 450 (effective pot odds ~2.8:1 post-call before BB acts). We're OOP but deep-stacked (94BB), so implied odds are strong if we hit. Folding is too tight in SB vs BTN; 3-betting is possible as a light 3-bet but better to realize equity by calling and controlling pot size. BB (Flash) may squeeze or fold, but we defend wide here to maximize stack growth.

I am in the big blind with 8-5 offsuit, a very weak starting hand. Minni raised from the button and Elon called from the small blind. While I am getting 4.5-to-1 pot odds to call the remaining 200 chips, I will be out of position for the rest of the hand against two opponents. 8-5 offsuit does not realize its equity well in multi-way pots and often leads to difficult decisions on later streets. I will play it safe and fold this hand.

Flop of 5c-2s-4s is a dry, low board with two spades, favorable for Minni's BTN opening range which includes many Ax suited, suited connectors, pocket pairs, and bluffs. I have Ah7s: Ace-high with the 7s giving a weak backdoor spade draw and no pair, straight draw, or flush draw equity (only one spade in hand). No gutshot or open-ender; pure air but with A blocker. OOP as SB, betting out is a donk bet that's suboptimal without a strong range advantage. Checking allows pot control and folding to a likely c-bet from aggressive Minni (history shows frequent continuation betting). No showdown value if checked through, and reverse implied odds if I float/call. Check-fold maximizes EV long-term by avoiding spew against her polarized range. Deep stacks don't justify hero-calling air.

I opened preflop with 3c3d and SB called. Flop 5c-2s-4s: I currently have a small/middle pair (pair of threes) — decent showdown value vs unpaired overcards but behind any 5x/4x/2x and vulnerable to draws. As the preflop aggressor in position this is a good spot for a small continuation bet to (1) fold out overcards, (2) charge spade/straight draws, and (3) extract value from worse hands while keeping the pot controllable. I will bet ~1/3 pot; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn, fold to large polarized raises, and look to extract more if I improve.

Facing a 250 bet into 950 on a dry, low, spade-heavy flop (5c-2s-4s) as OOP in SB with Ah7s (ace-high, backdoor spade draw, nut flush blocker but no pair or strong equity). Minni's button range hits this board decently (suited spades, low connectors/pairs, wheels), and she c-bets frequently (~70% from history). Pot odds ~3.8:1 but equity too low (<30% vs her range) for a call/float OOP; no showdown value and poor implied odds if behind. Raising as bluff possible with blockers but OOP vs aggressive BTN risks floats/check-raises; folding air preserves stack for better spots in 3-handed deep tournament.

Preflop on the button with 5d3h offsuit, a very weak hand with poor playability, low equity, and no strong potential draws or made hands possible. Pot is 150 after blinds, to call 100 for ~1/3 pot odds but with trash equity against any defense. Position is good but this hand folds to any raise and doesn't play well postflop. Deep stacks, 3-handed, but preserve stack for premium spots. Standard fold to avoid speculative plays.

I am in the Small Blind with a small pocket pair (33). Since the Button has folded, it is just me and the Big Blind (Minni). 33 is a strong enough hand to play heads-up. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or set myself up to win on the flop.

Facing a 300 SB open heads-up with K♠6♣ in the big blind. With Elon folded this is a two‑handed pot and Flash (SB) will have postflop position, so 3‑betting a marginal K6o is unnecessary and often dominated. Folding is too tight for the cheap price. Calling the 200 extra (300 total committed including my BB) gives good pot odds to see a flop and lets me proceed cautiously out of position: continue with top pair or strong draws, otherwise check/fold to sustained aggression. I’ll call and evaluate the flop.

I hold pocket threes on a board of Queen-Ten-Seven rainbow. This board texture is quite unfavorable for a small pair as it hits many of the Big Blind's calling ranges (hands like Qx, Tx, J9, or 89). As the pre-flop aggressor, I could consider a continuation bet, but given the coordination of the board and the likelihood that Minni has a piece of it, I prefer to check and see how she reacts. I am prepared to fold to most significant bets on this flop.

I called preflop heads-up with K♠6♣ and the flop comes Q♥ T♣ 7♠. I have a single overcard and essentially no draw — very little equity unless I hit a King. Flash (the preflop raiser) checked in position, which can be either weakness or pot control with a made hand. Betting here would be a pure bluff with limited fold equity against a wide but calling-heavy opening range (Qx, Tx, pairs, floats). Out of position I don’t want to build the pot with near-zero equity. I’ll check to control the pot, see a free turn, and then fold to meaningful aggression unless I pair up or get a favorable card/reading to continue.

On the turn, the board is Tc 7s Qh 4h. My pocket threes have very little showdown value and are likely behind any pair or even some high-card combinations. After checking the flop and seeing Minni check back, her range is likely capped, but she still has many hands that beat mine (any 7, any 10, or any Queen). Betting here would be a pure bluff, and since the flop went check-check, she might be more inclined to call a turn bet with any piece of the board. I will check again to keep the pot small and look to get to a cheap showdown or fold if she shows strength.

I have K♠6♣ on Q♥ T♣ 7♠ 4♥ — no pair, no meaningful draw (no hearts, no straight draw). Flash raised preflop and has position but checked both flop and turn, which looks like pot control or a marginal made hand; betting out here would be a pure bluff with very limited fold equity and would bloat the pot out of position. Pot is only 600 and effective stacks are deep, so I should preserve chips and keep options for the river. I’ll check and plan to fold to any significant bet on the river, only consider a river bluff if a very favorable card or read appears.

The river brings a second Ten to the board (Tc 7s Qh 4h Td). Given that both Minni and I have checked the flop and turn, it is highly unlikely that either of us holds a Queen or a Ten. My pocket threes have very little showdown value, but they can occasionally win against missed straight draws like 98 or 65. I will check to see a free showdown. If Minni bets, I will likely fold as I have no reason to believe my pair of threes is good here.

I have K♠6♣ on Q♥ T♣ 7♠ 4♥ T♦ — no pair, no meaningful draw. Flash was the preflop raiser and checked flop and turn, which reads as pot-control with a made hand (Qx, Tx, pocket pair) or a cautious line; either way he’s unlikely to fold a river bet very often. The pot is small (600) relative to deep stacks, so a bluff would need to be sizable to get fold equity and is unjustified here. I’ll check and preserve chips; I’ll fold to any significant bet on the river.
I am on the Button with AK suited, which is a premium starting hand, especially in a 3-handed game. I will make a standard opening raise of 3x the big blind to build the pot and assert initiative.

5♠2♦ is one of the weakest starting hands. Facing a button open to 300, I’d have to call 250 more (300 total) into a 450 pot and will be out of position with poor post‑flop playability and almost no equity vs a typical opening range. The big blind still has to act, so this is likely to become multiway or face a 3‑bet — not a spot I want to invest chips from my big stack with junk. I could consider an aggressive 3‑bet bluff in some situations, but against two opponents with ~9–10k stacks it’s unnecessary risk when I can wait for a much better spot. Fold and preserve my deep stack for higher EV opportunities.

Preflop in the big blind facing a button raise to 300 (200 to call into 450 pot). My hand is J3o, offsuit and unconnected with poor playability and low equity against a wide button opening range (typically 40-50% of hands). While pot odds are decent (~2.25:1), J3o is near the bottom of any reasonable defending range due to being easily dominated postflop and lacking straight/flush potential. Opponent Flash has shown aggression from late position in prior hands but no specific read justifies defending this trash hand. Folding preserves stack for better spots in a long tournament (hand 30/150).

Button with 7♣4♥ — a weak hand, but short-handed and in position. Opening wide from the button is standard to steal the blinds and take the initiative. I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB-ish), a normal open size here: it maximizes fold equity, avoids giving the blinds a cheap flop, and keeps pot control with a marginal hand. Plan: fold to a big 3-bet, otherwise play postflop aggressively with position (c-bet when favorable, pot control or check back when board is bad for my holdings).

Preflop in the small blind facing a button raise to 300 (to call 250 into 450 pot). My hand is 3s Td, offsuit trash with poor connectivity, dominated against any reasonable button opening range from Minni. Button ranges are wide, but T3o has very low equity OOP, especially multiway potential with BB behind. Pot odds are about 2:1 effective (250 to win 700), but long-term EV is negative due to poor playability postflop. Deep stacks (90BB), no need to defend this wide. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind holding Jack-Deuce offsuit. Minni, who has a large stack and has been playing aggressively, raised from the Button to 300. Although I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win 450), Jack-Deuce is a very weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, especially when out of position. Given that I have 120 hands left in the tournament and need to protect my stack, there is no reason to defend with such a marginal hand against a button open. Folding is the most disciplined play here.

Preflop on the button with 3 players left, facing blinds only. My hand is K3o, a marginal king-high offsuit hand with position advantage. With deep stacks (90BB effective), the button opening range is wide to steal blinds frequently. Opponent history shows many folds to raises (e.g., Hands 9,10,13,15,19,22,25,30,31), so raising to steal the 150 pot is +EV. Standard size 3x BB to 300. If called or 3bet, can fold postflop due to poor playability. Limping is weak and invites multiway pots; folding is too tight from button.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Six offsuit. Elon has opened from the Button for 300. J6o is a very weak hand to defend from the Small Blind, especially since I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. While ranges are wider in 3-handed play, this hand does not have enough equity or playability to justify a call or a 3-bet. Folding is the most profitable long-term decision here.

Elon opened to 300 from the button; I’m in the big blind with 7♦9♦. Pot is 450 and I need to add 200 (300 total) to see the flop. 9-7 suited is a good defensive hand heads-up: it has strong playability, makes straights/flushes, and gets excellent implied odds versus a wide button opening range. Calling keeps the pot size manageable while preserving stack depth — 3-betting OOP with this speculative hand is too thin and will often put me in uncomfortable, bloated spots. Pot odds to call are ~31% break-even, and my hand’s equity versus a typical button open is generally better than that. Plan: see the flop; proceed aggressively with strong draws or made hands (especially diamond runs), check-call small c-bets to realize equity, and fold to large turn river aggression when I miss. Action: call to 300 total.

I hold 9♦7♦ on A♠ 7♣ 6♠ — a vulnerable middle pair (7s) with a 9 kicker and only backdoor diamond potential. Elon was the preflop raiser and now checked the flop, which represents relative weakness (missed Ax, broadways, or a small pocket pair). Against a checked flop I should take the initiative: a value/protection bet will get calls from worse pairs, Kx/Qx/broadways that missed, and some draws, while folding out pure air and denying free cards that can improve over me. I’ll size to roughly half the pot to extract value without committing too much — large enough to charge overcards but small enough to fold out bluffs and keep worse hands in. Plan: bet ~1/2 pot (325). If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he check-raises big I’ll respect the aggression and fold to avoid playing a bloated pot with a marginal pair.

Preflop button raise with K3o was a standard steal attempt given position and opponents' folding tendencies. On this Ace-high flop (As7c6s), my hand has zero equity: no pair, no flush draw (Kd3c), no straight draw (possible gutshot unlikely), and King-high is crushed by villain's BB calling range which includes Ax, pairs, suited connectors that connect here. Pot 975, her small cbet of 325 into it smells like Ax or draw/value, but regardless, I have <20% equity needed for pot odds call (325 into 1300 ~25%, but realistically much worse). Raising as a bluff is bad: poor blockers (K blocks some AK but not Ax), reverse position, deep stacks (90BB), and she calls stations often (see hands 1,3,6,14,20,23 where she calls big). Folding weak air from wide stealing range maintains image and preserves stack for better spots. Long-term +EV.

I am on the button with Ten-Deuce offsuit (T2o), which is a very weak hand. In a three-handed game, while positional advantage is important, T2o does not have enough equity or playability to justify an open-raise or a call, especially with a large stack like Minni in the Small Blind who has been active and Elon in the Big Blind. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

7♠4♦ offsuit is one of the weakest hands. I'm out of position against the big blind with the button folded; I don't want to inflate the pot or face post‑flop decisions with virtually no equity. Folding surrenders only my small blind (50) and preserves my deep stack to pick better spots later. So I fold.

Button with Q♥9♥ — a playable, suited hand with good post‑flop playability and blockers. Pot is 150 and it’s only 100 to call, but I want to take the initiative and steal the blinds/isolate heads‑up. Both opponents are reasonably deep but have shown a mix of folds and calls to steals; a standard 3xBB sizing (~300 total) is large enough to generate fold equity yet keeps the pot manageable if called. Plan: raise to 300 to play the hand in position. If I get a single call I’ll c‑bet favorable flops and use position to extract value or fold to heavy aggression on very bad boards. If I face a large 3‑bet I will fold Q9s rather than get jocularly involved out of position.

Hand #34, preflop in SB with 2d Jc offsuit, a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity against Minni's button raise range, which appears aggressive based on history (frequent raises from all positions). Pot odds to call 250 into 450 (~2.4:1) are not sufficient given the hand's trash equity (~20-30% vs typical button open), multiway potential with BB behind, and my position. Stacks are deep (87bb effective vs Minni), so folding preserves stack for better spots in this 3-handed tournament phase. Standard fold to avoid spew.

I am in the Big Blind with 2c 3c, a low suited connector. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. The pot is currently 450 and it costs me 200 to call. I am getting 3.25-to-1 odds on a call. While 23s is a weak hand, in a three-handed game against a wide Button opening range, defending the Big Blind at these odds is standard to remain competitive and not allow the Button to steal blinds too easily. I have a comfortable stack of 100 big blinds to play post-flop.

I have completely missed the flop with 2c 3c on a monotone diamond board (4d Qd 9d). I have no diamonds in my hand, meaning I have zero equity and no relevant blockers to continue. I am first to act post-flop. The standard and most prudent play here is to check and fold to any aggression from Minni.

Flop: Q♦9♦4♦ with Q♥9♥ in hand — I have two pair (Q9), a very strong made hand, but the board is monotone (three diamonds) so any opponent holding two diamonds has an immediate flush. The BB called my 3x preflop, so his range is wide and contains many one-diamond hands (a single diamond = a 4-flush) and only a small proportion of two-diamond combos (made flops flushes are rare). I want to extract value from worse pairs and charge one-diamond draws while denying a free diamond on the turn, so I’ll continuation-bet a meaningful, non-tiny size (~60% pot). If I face a large raise I will give credit for a made flush and fold — but vs a call I can proceed and re-evaluate on the turn (protect against diamonds, capitalize on non-diamond blanks).

I have 2c 3c on a 4d Qd 9d board. I have no diamonds, no pair, and no straight draws. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range which includes many broadway diamonds and high pairs. Minni has bet 400 into a 650 pot. With zero equity and no potential to improve to the best hand, continuing would be a significant mistake. I will fold.

3-handed on the button first to act preflop with 8h3h suited. This is a speculative hand with straight and flush potential, well-suited for multiway pots or stealing blinds from position. Opponents' stacks are deep (mine 87BB), and history shows frequent preflop raises to 300, which has been effective. Raising to 3x BB (300) takes initiative, applies fold equity against blinds (Minni and Flash often defend but not always), and positions me well postflop if called. Folding is too tight; limping passive. Standard button raise.

In a 3-handed game, JTo is a decent hand, but being in the Small Blind is the worst position. Elon's button open to 3x represents a standard wide range. While I am getting decent pot odds to call, playing out of position against Elon, with the very aggressive chip leader Minni still to act in the Big Blind, makes a call risky. Minni has shown a high propensity to squeeze or play aggressively post-flop. Folding here allows me to preserve my stack and wait for a more advantageous situation where I have either a stronger hand or better position.

Facing a button raise to 300 from the big blind with Kc2h. In heads-up play Kx is worth defending — folding is too tight and a small 3-bet with this raggy hand just bloats the pot or invites a 4-bet. Pot is 450 and I need to call 200 more (total commitment will be 300), so I’d need ~31% equity to break even — defendible against a wide opening range. Plan: call to see a flop, play cautiously out of position, look to realize equity or fold to heavy action if I miss.

I defended K2 preflop and the flop (5♦-2♦-4♣) gives me a made pair of deuces with a King kicker. I’m out of position against a wide button raising range; the board is low and relatively dry but with two diamonds (some draw potential). This is a hand to pot-control and realize equity rather than bloating the pot OOP. I’ll check—induce a c-bet and call reasonable sizing to extract value from worse and deny free cards to pure floats. If Elon checks back I’m happy to see a turn for free; if he barrels small/standard I’ll call and re-evaluate on the turn; if he applies heavy pressure (pot-sized or larger) I’ll fold to preserve my deep stack against likely two-pair/overpair or strong draws.

Flop board 5d-2d-4c hits some straight draws (wheel gutshots like mine with 3h, 6x open-enders) and diamond flush draws, but my button raising range (A-high, suited connectors, broadways) hits this medium board reasonably often with overpairs, top pair, or draws. Minni defended wide from BB by calling my button raise preflop and checked, showing weakness. Position allows me to c-bet for value/thin value with my gutshot (4 outs to wheel) while charging draws, and high fold equity against her checking range. Pot 650, betting ~60% pot (400) balances bluffs/value, denies equity, builds pot if called with equity. Checking risks giving free card to draws or letting her repop. Standard continuation bet from initiative.

I defended K2 preflop and flopped a made pair of deuces (K kicker). Elon’s bet is small relative to the pot (400 into 1050 ≈ 28% immediate pot odds), and his button opening/c-bet range is wide — many bluffs and single-pair/overcard hands that I beat. Out of position I want to control the pot and realize equity rather than inflate it with a marginal 3‑bet. I’ll call to extract value from worse and re‑evaluate on the turn; fold if faced with large pot-sized raises that represent two‑pair/trips or completed draws.

I have K♣2♥ — bottom pair (deuces) with a decent king kicker, out of position. The turn Q♣ pairs the board and adds a club, but with only three clubs between my hand and the board a flush is impossible on the river, so my hand remains marginal. Elon checked after c-betting the flop, which narrows his range toward Qx, some overcards, missed draws and occasional traps. Betting OOP would bloat the pot and expose me to check-raises or committing against better hands for thin value. I’ll pot‑control and realize equity: check and see the river. Plan is to call reasonable river bets for pot odds, fold to big river aggression, and reassess if checked through to showdown.

Turn board brings Qc, which adds a club draw but doesn't complete many strong hands. I hold 3h8h for an open-ended straight draw (A or 6, 8 outs, ~32% equity heads-up), plus backdoor heart flush draw. Minni checked flop and turn, indicating weakness or trapping, but her preflop defend and flop call suggest a wide range including floats, weak pairs, draws. With position, deep stacks (80BB effective vs her 208BB), and initiative, semi-bluff bet exploits fold equity while building pot for when I hit. Pot 1450; betting ~2/3 pot (1000) balances value from her Qx/mid pairs/draws and pressure. Checking risks giving free river card to improve her equity or letting her bluff. Standard aggressive line continuation.

I have K♣2♥ — bottom pair (deuces) with a decent king kicker, out of position. The pot is 2450 and calling 1000 gives ~29% pot odds (1000/(2450+1000) ≈ 29%), so a call only needs ~29% equity. Elon’s line (preflop raise, small c-bet, turn bet of 1000) can include a lot of Qx/overcards, some value hands and a fair number of bluffs/missed draws that I currently beat. I only have ~5 clean outs to improve (3 Ks + 2 deuces ≈ 11%), but the hand has reasonable showdown value against his wide turning range. Raising OOP with a marginal one‑pair hand would bloat the pot and invite a shove; folding gives up too much given the pot odds. Plan: call to see the river, fold to large polarizing raises/shoves, call modest river bets for value, and only re‑raise if I improve or get a clear read that he’s bluffing.

I hold K♣2♥ — bottom pair (deuces) with a decent king kicker. The river (5♦-2♦-4♣-Q♣-6♣) introduces some danger (any 3 in villain’s hand makes a 2–6 straight; two clubs in villain’s hand would complete a flush), but Elon’s line (preflop open, small c-bet on the flop, a turn bet of 1000, then checking the river) looks more like pot‑control or a missed/medium-strength hand rather than a thin value shove on the river. I beat a lot of his missed overcards and bluffs and have reasonable showdown value; betting now would either fold out worse hands or get raised by better hands (Qx, straights, flushes), which I don’t want to face with a marginal one‑pair hand out of position. With no bet to call and good showdown prospects, the lowest-variance, highest-expected-value line is to check to showdown.

River board completes the 2-3-4-5-6 straight with my 3h8h, giving me a strong six-high straight. Possible club flush (three clubs on board: 4c, Qc, 6c) beats it, but Minni's passive check-call lines on flop (400) and turn (1000) without raising suggest she lacks the flush or a monster; likely holds a queen, mid-pair, two-pair, or missed draw. My button raising range is perceived as strong, justifying a value bet. Pot is 3450; betting ~60% pot (2000) extracts value from her calling range (Qx+, sets unlikely) while risking little relative to stack (7000). Avoids checking and potentially missing value or facing a bluff-raise.

I hold K♣2♥ — bottom pair (deuces) with a decent K kicker. After Elon’s river bet the pot is 5450 and I must call 2000, so I’m getting ~27% pot odds (2000 / (5450+2000) ≈ 26.9%). Elon’s line (button open, small flop c‑bet, continued turn bet, moderate river bet) is polarized: it contains value (Qx, 6x/5x, sets, straights with a 3, or a two‑club flush) but also a meaningful number of missed hands and bluffs given his earlier small flop sizing. My hand has reasonable showdown value versus a wide continuing range and meets the pot‑odds threshold, while raising OOP with a marginal pair would bloat the pot and be a losing line against his value combos. I’ll call to realize equity and see showdown; fold only to a large polarized shove, but don’t inflate the pot by raising here.

I am on the button with 6-4 offsuit. While 3-handed play requires opening a wide range of hands, 6-4 offsuit is too weak to profitably raise as a steal, especially given the stack sizes and the aggressiveness shown by Minni and Elon in previous hands. Folding is the standard and safest play here.

Heads-up from the small blind (I’m also the button postflop) with K♦8♥. Pot is 150 and it’s only Elon (BB) left — I’ve already posted 50 and need 50 to just call. Against a single opponent who defends fairly wide and plays aggressively postflop, I want the initiative and to use position. A standard heads-up open size of ~3x the BB (300 total) puts pressure on marginal big-blind hands while keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks. If Elon 3-bets large I can fold K8o; if he calls I’ll play the flop with the positional advantage. Raise to 300 (total).

Facing a standard 3x open from the SB after Flash folds on the button. A6o is a strong defending hand in the BB vs SB wide range due to the ace blocker, decent high card strength, and solid equity (~45% vs typical SB opening range). Pot odds are excellent (calling 200 into 400, ~3:1). Prefer calling over 3-betting as OOP playability is reasonable with top pair potential. Will proceed cautiously postflop, folding to heavy aggression without improvement.

I raised preflop and have position, but the flop 3h-7s-4d is very dry and my Kd8h has no pair and no real draw. Elon defends widely and has shown postflop aggression, so a c‑bet here risks getting check‑raised or bloating the pot with a marginal hand. I’ll check to control the pot, see how he reacts, and plan to call small bets to realize my overcard equity or fold to large pressure. If checked through I can continue on a safe turn (or bet for value if I hit a King).

Preflop call with A6o was standard defense vs SB wide range. Flop 3h7s4d is fairly dry and disconnected, missing me completely—no pair, no strong draw, just ace-high with a weak backdoor diamond flush draw. Minni's check from SB suggests weakness or trapping, but her range still includes overcards, gutshots, and weak pairs. As OOP, checking back is optimal for pot control, realizing equity for free on the turn, and avoiding difficult spots if I bet into a calling range or face a raise. Betting here would be thin value or a bluff without great blockers or fold equity justification given deep stacks and her tendency to check-call light in history. Will reassess turn.

Board on the turn: 3♥-7♠-4♦-9♥; my hand K♦8♥. I have no pair and no real draw — with only the river to come I cannot make a flush (only three hearts total between my hand and board), and there are very few clean outs (only Kings give me a pair). Elon defended wide preflop and can have a lot of medium pairs (4x,7x,9x), overcards, or showdown-y hands; he’s also shown the tendency to turn aggression into raises. Betting here would be a thin bluff that will often get called or check‑raised by stronger holdings, and I have essentially no value to extract. Pot is small relative to our stacks, so preserving chips and controlling the pot is best. I’ll check to keep the pot manageable, be willing to fold to a significant bet, and only call small river bets when pot odds justify it; if checked through I’ll re-evaluate the river and consider a bluff only with a favorable card or fold to pressure.

Facing Minni's second check on the turn after checking through the flop. Board is 3h7s4d9h, which is somewhat coordinated with possible straight draws (e.g., 56, 25-6 for wheel elements) and now two hearts bringing flush draw potential. My Ad6h is ace-high with a backdoor heart draw (6h), no pair, no strong draw, but the ace is a strong blocker to her top pair hands, and ace-high has good showdown value against her likely weak checking range from SB open (overcards, weak pairs like 88-JJ, gutshots, air). She has checked twice, indicating capped range without strong made hands, and history shows she check-calls light (e.g., Hands 14, 20, 35). Pot is 600, stacks deep (~20BB+). A small bet of ~2/3 pot extracts value from her worse hands (K-high, Q-high, weaker pairs like 55, 88) that she calls light, while having fold equity against pure air. Checking risks giving free card to draws or letting her realize equity cheaply, and pot control isn't critical with no pair. Betting builds pot when ahead and semi-bluffs missed draws from her range. Size 400 for good risk-reward.

Board: 3h-7s-4d-9h. My hand: Kd8h — no pair, no meaningful draw. I only have 6 clean outs (3 kings + 3 eights) → ~6/46 ≈ 13% equity to improve on the river. The pot is 1000 and it costs 400 to call, so the call is offering ~400/1400 ≈ 28.6% pot odds. My raw equity (≈13%) is well below the pot odds required, so a call is -EV. Elon’s line (check-check on the flop then a turn raise) is consistent with value (middle pairs like 7x/4x/9x or Kx/overcards turned into a bluff sometimes), and he has a history of turning aggression into raises. Raising as a bluff is an option, but I lack sufficient blockers and would often face a call or a shove from better holdings — it’s a thin, high-variance play I don’t need to take with 17,500 chips and deep stacks remaining. Pot control and fold are the prudent plays here. I’ll fold and wait for a better spot in position.

4♣8♦ is a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with little showdown value, but I’m on the button with deep stacks and good fold equity against the blinds. The table’s default open-raise size has been ~300, so I’ll raise to 300 to steal the blinds and keep initiative — avoiding a limp that invites multiway pots where this hand performs poorly. If I get called I’ll proceed cautiously postflop (small continuation bet on favorable textures, check/fold to strong resistance); if I face a 3-bet I’ll fold unless the price or reads justify continuing.

Jd6d is a marginal suited connector from the small blind facing a button raise to 300 (3x). Button opening ranges are wide (likely 35-50% of hands), but J6s has poor equity against that range (~35-40%), limited playability due to low cards, and poor implied odds. Pot odds are ~1.8:1 (calling 250 into 450), but postflop EV is negative given position disadvantage and reverse implied odds if I hit. Standard GTO play is to fold; preserve stack for better spots in a deepstack tournament (hand 37/150). No strong history read justifies a hero call or 3bet.

I am in the Big Blind with 8s5d, facing a 3x open (300) from Minni on the Button. The pot is 450 and it costs me 200 to call, giving me pot odds of 2.25:1. While these odds are decent, 85 offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially against a Button opening range which, while wide, often contains high cards that dominate my low cards. My stack is healthy at nearly 100 big blinds, and I don't need to defend with such a weak hand out of position. I'll fold and wait for a better spot.

Preflop on the button with Js4s, a suited hand with straight and flush potential. The pot is 150 with blinds posted (likely SB limped 50, BB 100), facing a call of 100 for great implied odds in position with deep stacks. Multi-way pots favor speculative suited hands like this postflop, where I can realize equity cheaply. Folding is too tight; raising is not standard with this hand facing a limper. Limping in position exploits implied odds against calling stations like Minni, who has shown frequent calls in history.

I am in the Small Blind holding Queen of Clubs and Nine of Diamonds. Elon has limped from the button for 100. The pot currently has 250 chips (100 from Minni, 100 from Elon, and 50 from my small blind). I only need to contribute 50 more chips to see the flop, giving me 5-to-1 pot odds. Q9o is a strong enough hand to complete the bet from this position, even though I will be playing out of position. Calling allows me to see a flop cheaply without overcommitting with a marginal hand.

I’m in the big blind with Ad2d and two opponents have limped (pot 300). Ad2d has good playability — blocker to the nut Ace, nut-flush potential and straight/backdoor equity — and I want to avoid a cheap three-way flop where my hand is often dominated. A small minimum raise (to 200) would keep too many players in; I prefer to isolate or take the pot now. A raise to 1,200 (≈4x the pot) applies meaningful fold equity against the small blind and pressures the button’s calling range while still leaving plenty of stack behind to maneuver postflop. If I get called, I can c-bet many flops or proceed cautiously on Ace-high/connected textures; if I face a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold vs a polarised shove/very large 3-bet, or call/4-bet depending on sizing and reads). Overall this sizing maximizes value and fold equity in this spot.

Js4s is a speculative suited hand with position on the raiser. Preflop limp was for multiway implied odds, but now facing a raise to 1200 (12BB) after two limps. Pot odds are favorable: calling 1100 into a pot that will be ~2500+ heads-up (or more if Flash calls), offering ~2.3:1 or better. Equity vs. Minni's likely wide BB raising range after limps (pairs, Ax, suited connectors, broadway) is around 35-40%, sufficient with implied odds given deep stacks (12k effective) and Minni's history of frequent postflop calls as a calling station. Position allows controlling pot and realizing equity postflop with flush/straight potential. Folding is too tight; 3-betting is suboptimal with a bluff catcher that plays poorly postflop when called. Calling keeps pot multiway possible if Flash continues (unlikely but adds value), exploits Minni's tendencies, and maintains stack growth trajectory in tournament.

Minni has made a very large squeeze to 1200 from the Big Blind after a limp and a call. Although I have already invested 100 chips, calling an additional 1100 with Queen-Nine offsuit puts me in a difficult spot. I would be playing a large pot out of position against two opponents, one of whom has shown significant strength. My hand is likely dominated by Minni's raising range and Elon's calling range. Preserving my stack for better opportunities is the better long-term strategy.

I raised preflop to isolate and am now heads-up vs Elon on a Jh Ts 3s flop. I hold Ad2d: no pair, Ace-high only, but I have the Ace blocker and a backdoor diamond. Elon’s calling range to my preflop raise will contain lots of broadways, connectors and medium pairs plus some spade draws. As the preflop aggressor I should continuation-bet a wide portion of my range to take away equity from floats and fold out many Kx/Qx and small pairs while still having maneuverability post‑call. A small-to-medium c-bet (~30–40% pot) achieves good fold equity against his missed hands and keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks; it also prices in spade draws rather than giving them a free card. I choose to bet 900 into the 2500 pot (~36%) — if he folds I win the pot; if he calls I can re-evaluate on the turn (fire a second barrel on favorable cards like an Ace or blank that advantageously polarizes my perceived range, otherwise control size or check behind); if he raises significantly I will usually fold given my lack of pair and the strength required to raise here.

Flop of J♥ T♠ 3♠ hits me perfectly with top pair (pair of jacks using Js) and nut flush draw (Js4s, four spades to nut flush). Board is draw-heavy with two spades and open-enders possible. Heads-up in position vs Minni's BB raise-then-cbet range (Jx, Tx, 88-QQ, Ax, flush draws, sets/straights). Equity ~65-75% vs her polarized continuation range due to strong made hand + draw combo. Raising for value from weaker pairs/draws/overs, protection against her draws, and pot-building while deep-stacked. Calling risks giving free cards or smaller pots OOP; shoving too aggressive for tournament ICM/stack preservation. Size ~75% of pot (3400) at 2500 total extracts max value, nice round number fitting bet history, leaves room for postflop play. Exploits Minni's calling station tendencies from history while growing stack aggressively but balanced.

Elon flat-called preflop and now 3‑bets my 900 c‑bet to 2,500 on Jh Ts 3s. I hold Ad2d: Ace‑high with an Ace blocker and backdoor diamonds, but no pair and no spade draw. My plan going in was to c‑bet wide but to fold to a significant raise on this texture — that’s exactly what this line looks like. Calling 1,600 here requires ~21.3% equity (1,600/(5,900+1,600)), and versus a raising range that is heavily weighted toward Jx/Tx/sets/overpairs and two‑spade semi‑bluffs my raw equity is well below that unless Elon is extremely loose/aggressive. Raising as a bluff (min to 4,100) would commit a lot more chips with little fold equity against a polarized value range. With deep stacks and three players left I’d rather preserve chips and wait for a better spot, so I fold.

I am on the button with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a premium hand in 3-handed play. Standard strategy dictates an open-raise here to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. Given the previous betting patterns in this tournament, a raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard and effective sizing.

I have J♠T♠ in the small blind facing a standard button open to 300 with deep stacks (I have 15500). JTs is a strong, playable 3‑handed hand with good equity and postflop playability. A 3‑bet OOP here is marginal — I’d be out of position vs the button and could get called by the big blind — so I prefer pot control and flexibility. Calling 250 (300 total commitment) keeps the pot manageable, preserves implied odds to hit straights/flushes, and lets me see a flop where I can continue on favorable textures or fold to heavy action if I miss. I will call to 300 and then evaluate the flop before committing more chips.

Preflop in the big blind facing a button raise to 300 and a small blind call. Pot is 700, calling 200 more offers excellent pot odds of 3.5:1 (roughly 22% equity required). 6s9c is offsuit but has decent playability with straight potential (5-7-8, 7-8-10, open-enders), flush draw blockers, and two-pair possibilities. Multiway pot expected, but implied odds are strong with deep stacks (148BB effective). Button range is wide (~25-35%), SB call even wider. Folding is too tight in BB; raising unprofitable with this hand. Call to realize equity and control postflop from position.

I have J♠T♠ on a T♥-7♣-4♥ flop — top pair with a very strong jack kicker in a 3‑way pot. The preflop raiser (button) and the big blind will both have wide ranges that include worse tens, medium pairs, and many heart combo draws. Betting is best here to get value from worse tens/medium pairs and to charge/price out heart draws and overcards. I choose a half‑pot bet (~450) — big enough to protect and extract, small enough to keep worse hands and draws in to call. If I face a large raise I’ll re‑evaluate (call small raises for pot control, fold to polarized big 3‑bets that look like sets/quads).

Preflop call was correct with 6s9c for pot odds multiway from BB. Flop 7c4hTh completely misses my hand: no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw (gutshot potential at best with 6, but insignificant). Minni's SB lead into two players for half-pot sizing suggests strength: likely pairs (77,44,TT,87s etc.), heart draws (any two hearts), straight draws (56,85, JQ etc.), or sets. My equity is poor, around 15-20% against her range even heads-up, worse multiway with Flash behind. Pot odds 3:1 require 25% equity, which I lack. Calling OOP invites further aggression from Flash or Minni; better to fold and preserve stack for better spots. No fold equity to raise.

I have Ace-Jack offsuit on a flop of 10-7-4. While I missed the board, I have two overcards (Ace and Jack) which likely provide six outs to the best hand if Minni has a pair of tens or lower. Minni's lead of 450 into 900 (half pot) is a common probe bet or protection bet. With pot odds of 3:1 and the advantage of position, calling is a standard and profitable play here. It allows me to see a turn card and potentially realize my equity or take the pot away if Minni checks on the next street.

I have top pair (T) with a strong J‑kicker, but the turn (6♥) completes a three‑heart board (T♥‑7♣‑4♥‑6♥). Flash check‑called my half‑pot flop bet, so his range includes worse tens, medium pairs and heart draws — and now any two hearts in his hand have turned into a made flush. With no heart blockers and being out of position, I don’t want to inflate the pot into a spot where a simple raise usually represents a completed flush or a slow‑played set. I’ll check to control the pot and to induce bluffs; if Flash bets a small amount (≈≤½ pot ≈900) I’ll call and reevaluate the river, but I’ll fold to a larger, value‑heavy raise which is likely a flush/set given the texture and previous action.

Minni's check on the turn, following her lead on the flop, often indicates a marginal hand that is now concerned by the coordinated board texture, which includes a possible flush and several straights. As the pre-flop aggressor who called the flop bet, I can credibly represent a strong range here. A bet of 1000 into the 1800 pot applies significant pressure on her likely holdings like middle pairs or weak Tens. This allows me to take the pot down now or maintain initiative heading into the river, while still retaining six outs with my overcards.

I hold J♠T♠ — top pair with a very good kicker. The turn 6♥ makes the board T♥-4♥-6♥ (three hearts), so any two‑heart combo in Flash’s range has now converted to a made flush. Flash opened on the button, check‑called my half‑pot flop bet and is now betting the turn 1000 into a 2800 pot — a relatively small, probing sizing (~36% pot) that can be both value (made flushes/sets) and a blocker/probe/bluff. Folding top pair to a small turn bet would be too passive; raising OOP is unattractive since I have no heart blockers and could inflate the pot against completed flushes. Calling 1000 gives me ~26% pot‑equity break‑even (1000/(2800+1000) ≈26%), keeps weaker tens and bluffs in, controls the pot size, and lets me reevaluate the river. Plan: call 1000 now; on a heart or a large polarized shove on the river I will likely fold, while on a non‑heart river I can seek value or call smaller bets for showdown.

Board is 7c-4h-Th-6h-2s and I hold J♠T♠ — top pair with the best kicker I can reasonably have here but no heart blocker. Flash’s line (button open preflop, check‑call my half‑pot flop bet, then a small turn bet) is consistent with a polarized/made range: completed heart flushes, some sets/two‑pairs, and some weaker tens/bluffs. The turn already put three hearts on board, so many of his callers/flatters on the flop that contained two hearts have a made flush now. The river 2♠ is a blank relative to the heart threat and doesn’t improve my hand vs those value hands. Betting for thin value is unattractive because flushes will happily call and many worse hands will fold; raising is especially poor without heart blockers since it only builds the pot when I’m likely behind. I’ll check and go to showdown — plan to call a small river probe (reasonable small sizing) but fold to a large polarized shove that represents a clear made flush/set.

The river is the 2 of spades, which is a blank card. The board texture is coordinated with three hearts and potential straights (5-8 or 8-9). After Minni check-called my 1000 bet on the turn, she likely has some showdown value, such as a pair of Tens, a Seven, or a draw that she isn't willing to let go of. While my Ace-high has very little showdown value, checking behind is the most prudent play. Blurring here is risky because Minni's turn call suggests she has a hand she wants to take to showdown, and it's unlikely she will fold a Ten or better for a single river bet given the pot size. I will check and hope my Ace-high is somehow good or simply minimize my losses on this hand.

I'm on the button with Ts5s — a suited, playable hand with backdoor straight/flush potential. In a 3-handed pot I want the initiative and to apply pressure to the blinds, so I’ll open a standard 3x BB sizing to take fold equity and avoid giving cheap flops. Raising to 300 is a small investment relative to my 17.6k stack (~1.7%) but narrows fields and isolates the BB. Plan: fold to a large, decisive 3-bet (too strong for T5s); vs a single caller I’ll continuation-bet thin on dry, low/paired boards and exercise pot control or check when faced with big overcards (A/K) or heavy resistance. Overall this is a positional open-raise to seize initiative.

Preflop in SB facing a button raise to 300 (3BB) from Minni, who has shown frequent aggression in previous hands with wide ranges. My hand Ah8s is suited with an ace blocker, excellent playability, and strong potential against a polarized button opening range. Folding is too tight given pot odds (calling 250 into 450, ~36% equity needed, and A8s crushes many Ax worse and suited connectors). Flat calling risks multiway pot OOP with BB Flash likely to defend his stack. 3-betting to 900 (3x the raise) applies pressure, denies equity to worse Ax/hands, and allows me to take initiative OOP. If called, I have good postflop equity deep-stacked. Standard pro play to mix in these spots to maximize EV over tournament.

I am in the Big Blind with Pocket Tens (TT). Minni (Button) opened to 300, and Elon (Small Blind) 3-bet to 900. TT is a very strong hand, but playing it out of position against a 3-bet can be tricky.
